← Prepende · logbook

The most-likely build, animated

A coupled-oscillator Ising machine — the cheapest physical realization of the mechanism. This is a concept animation and an order-of-magnitude estimate, not a photograph of a working device and not a measurement.

What the bench does (each "solve")

phase 0   phase π   — anti-phase across an edge = that edge is cut. The sub-harmonic injection lock pins phases to {0, π}; the coupling drives edges to anti-phase.

One settle ≈ one restart. Most settles land a good cut; the exact optimum is rare and gets rarer with size (measured: hit-rate ~9% at n=80, → 0% by n≈100). So a real bench must run many restarts per verified solution — and that restart count is the crux of the calculation below.

What we can calculate from this model (estimates)

The animation makes the energy question concrete. Per verified solution:

E_solution ≈ N · P_osc · t_settle · R
  N = oscillators · P_osc = power each · t_settle = settle time · R = restarts per exact-optimum hit
These are order-of-magnitude estimates with wide error bars, not measurements. Plausible analog values (N≈80, P_osc≈1 mW, t_settle≈10 µs) give ~0.8 µJ per settle (R=1). At R≈10–20 restarts per verified solution, the per-solution cost is ~8–16 µJ — about an order of magnitude higher. R is the deciding term: our measured hit-rate (~9% at n=80, falling toward 0% by n≈100) implies R ≈ 10–20 at n=80 and growing with size. So energy per verified solution isn't a fixed per-settle figure — it scales with a restart count we have measured to climb.

The theoretical conclusion (and the next question it forces)

A fixed per-settle analog advantage over CPU simulated annealing is eroded — and at some size reversed — by the growing restart multiplier R. That implies a crossover size n*: below it the bench may win on energy per solution; above it the restart cost dominates and SA wins. We do not yet know n* (or whether it exists above trivial sizes). That is the next pre-registered question — drawn directly from this model, fed back into the ledger. The loop is the point: a model yields a calculation, the calculation yields a falsifiable question, the question goes on the record before we answer it.

Honest scope: every number here is an estimate from a software model; nothing on this page is a hardware measurement. The energy claim remains pre-registered prediction [T1], unresolved until a physical bench is built and metered.
